---
title: Mandatory Assignment 1
author: GFS189, RTL959
date: today
date-format: MMMM DD, YYYY
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
format: 
  pdf: 
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - top=20mm
      - left=20mm
      - bottom=20mm
      - right=20mm
    bibliography: references.bib
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike  
    lang: en
    fontsize: 12pt
    highlight-style: github-light
---



```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import tidyfinance as tf
from plotnine import * 
import tidyfinance as tf
from mizani.formatters import percent_format
import io
import re
import zipfile
from curl_cffi import requests
import os 
from joblib import Parallel, delayed, cpu_count
from itertools import product
from dateutil.relativedelta import relativedelta
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import pyarrow.dataset as ds
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
from scipy.optimize import minimize
from sklearn.covariance import LedoitWolf
from scipy.optimize import minimize
```


## Quarto - a framework for reproducible research

In this assigment we will look at the possiblility of using machine learning as a tool for financial gains through a model that predicts future returns of stocks. This assigment is inspired by the papers of @10.1093/rfs/hhaa009 and @10.1093/rfs/hhm014 which both explores how machine learning can used to predict stock returns. In the paper of @10.1093/rfs/hhaa009, they find, using a wide range of different machine learning model, that machine learning is a useful tool to predict stock returns and that the non-linear models outperform the linear models. Whereas the paper of @10.1093/rfs/hhm014 finds that machine learning models do not tend to outperform a simple historical mean model. Therefor we will try to reproduce the results of @10.1093/rfs/hhaa009. We do this by using the same data and machine learning models.

##
In this first part we will download the data which is stored in a parquet file and contains 94 stock characteristics from @10.1093/rfs/hhaa009 and 13 macroeconomic variables from @10.1093/rfs/hhm014, 73 industry classifications, excess returns and lagged market capitalization. We start by preparing the data by removing observations before January 2000. Now we select 10 stock characsterisc and 4 macroecoonmic variables based on the paper of @10.1093/rfs/hhaa009. Here we select 




```{python} 
#| echo: true
#| cache: true

ticker = ["AAPL", "MSFT", "BA"]
prices = tf.download_data(
  domain="stock_prices", 
  symbols=ticker,
  start_date="2000-01-01", 
  end_date="2025-12-31"
)

returns = (prices
  .assign(ret=lambda x: x.groupby("symbol")["adjusted_close"].pct_change())
  .get(["symbol", "date", "ret"])
  .dropna(subset="ret")
)
```

##

##

##

## Conclusion


## References
::::{#refs}
::::
